questions.q: Identify what a perceptron is and what it computes.
options: A complete neural network with input, hidden, and output layers that learns features automatically
A single neuron that computes a weighted sum of its inputs plus a bias, then applies an activation function to produce an output
A function that splits data into a training set and a test set to detect overfitting
An algorithm that pushes error backwards through the layers to adjust every weight
questions.explanation: A perceptron is the simplest possible neural network — a single neuron. It multiplies each input by a weight, adds them with a bias to form the weighted sum, and passes that sum through an activation function. Option A describes a multilayer network, not a single perceptron, and option D describes backpropagation; naming the weighted-sum-plus-bias-then-activation computation is what earns the mark.

questions.q: In a perceptron, what is the role of the weights and the bias?
options: The weights are the learned parameters that say how much each input matters, and the bias is an adjustable threshold
The weights store the network's predictions and the bias stores the correct labels
The weights set the number of layers and the bias sets the number of epochs
The weights are fixed by the programmer and the bias is calculated by the activation function
questions.explanation: The weights are the learned parameters — they express how much each input matters — and the bias acts as an adjustable threshold for the neuron. Option D is the common misconception that weights are hand-set; in fact fit() learns both the weights and the bias through the Predict-Loss-Adjust loop, which is exactly why they are called learned parameters.

questions.q: A perceptron has two inputs with weights 0.5 and 0.5, a bias of -0.7, and a step activation that outputs 1 if the sum is at least 0, otherwise 0. What does it output for inputs 1 and 1?
options: 0, because the weighted sum is -0.2 which is below 0
1, because the weighted sum is 0.3 which is at least 0
0, because the weighted sum is -0.7 which is below 0
1, because both inputs are 1 so the activation always fires
questions.explanation: The weighted sum is 0.5 times 1 plus 0.5 times 1, minus 0.7, which equals 0.3 — that is at least 0, so the step activation outputs 1. Option D is wrong because the perceptron does not fire simply because the inputs are 1; you must compute the weighted sum and compare it to the threshold. Multiply, sum, activate — show the working to earn the mark.

questions.q: Outline what the forward pass does and why it is called 'forward'.
options: It runs the inputs through the network — multiply by weights, sum with bias, apply the activation, layer by layer — because data flows forward from input to output
It pushes the error backwards through the layers to update each weight, working from output to input
It splits the dataset so that training data never overlaps with the test data
It repeatedly adjusts the weights over many epochs until the loss reaches zero
questions.explanation: The forward pass turns inputs into a prediction by running them through the network: multiply each input by its weight, sum with the bias, apply the activation, and feed each layer's output forward as the next layer's input. It is called 'forward' because data flows forward from input to output. The most tempting distractor describes backpropagation, which moves error backwards — the opposite direction.

questions.q: Describe the structure of a neural network.
options: A single layer of neurons that each compute a weighted sum, with no connections between them
A training set and a test set connected by a service worker that caches predictions

An input layer that receives raw features, one or more hidden layers that compute weighted sums and activations, and an output layer that produces the prediction, with neurons joined by weighted links
A chain of activation functions with no weights, where each function transforms the previous output
questions.explanation: A neural network has an input layer (one neuron per feature), one or more hidden layers in the middle, and an output layer that produces the final prediction, with neurons connected by weighted links where the knowledge lives. A single layer with no connections (option A) is wrong because the structure is multiple layers, and the hidden layers are what let a network model relationships a single perceptron never could — the early layers detect simple patterns and later layers combine them into complex features.

questions.q: The episode uses the hook P-L-A for the training loop. What do the three letters stand for?
options: Process, Learn, Apply
Parameters, Layers, Activations
Pass, Label, Average
Predict, Loss, Adjust
questions.explanation: P-L-A stands for Predict, Loss, Adjust: run a forward pass to predict, compare the output to the known label to measure the loss (how wrong it is), then nudge the weights to reduce that error — repeated over many epochs. The hand-wave answer 'it just learns the patterns' is a band-four non-answer; the marker wants this explicit loop, so reciting Predict-Loss-Adjust gives you the mechanism the question rewards.

questions.q: Explain how a neural network learns from data, using the terms introduced in the episode.
options: It processes the data through its layers and automatically learns the patterns without any measurable error step
It memorises every training example exactly so it can reproduce the correct label whenever that example appears again

For each example it runs a forward pass to predict, calculates the loss against the known label, uses backpropagation and gradient descent to adjust every weight to reduce the error, and repeats this over many epochs
It tries random weights until one combination happens to match the test set, then keeps that combination
questions.explanation: A network learns through repeated Predict-Loss-Adjust cycles: a forward pass produces a prediction, the loss measures how far it is from the label, backpropagation works out each weight's contribution to the error, and gradient descent adjusts the weights to reduce the loss, over many epochs. Option A is the hand-wave trap the episode warns against — it names no loss step and no adjustment, so it scores poorly. Memorising examples (option B) is overfitting, not learning.

questions.q: Distinguish between backpropagation and gradient descent as described in the episode.
options: Backpropagation works out how much each weight contributed to the error, while gradient descent steps the weights downhill in the direction that reduces the error
Backpropagation adjusts the weights downhill, while gradient descent measures how wrong the prediction was
Backpropagation is the forward pass and gradient descent is the loss calculation
They are two names for the same single step of running inputs through the network
questions.explanation: Backpropagation is the algorithm that pushes the error backwards through the layers to work out how much each weight contributed to the mistake; gradient descent is the step that nudges each weight downhill in the direction that reduces the error. Option B reverses the two roles. The episode stresses you can name both for polish, but the examinable substance is still the plain Predict-Loss-Adjust loop.

questions.q: A student writes that 'a neural network is just one big neuron, and the weights are neurons too.' Identify the vocabulary error and the correct distinction.
options: The statement is correct because every part of a network is a neuron
The error is confusing layers with epochs; a layer is one pass over the data

The error is muddling the terms; a neuron is a single unit that computes a weighted sum and activation, a layer is a group of neurons at the same stage, and a weight is the number on a connection that the network learns
The error is that weights are biases, and the bias is actually the activation function
questions.explanation: The episode names this muddling of vocabulary as a mark-costing trap: a neuron is a single unit (weighted sum plus activation), a layer is a group of neurons at the same stage, a weight is the learned number on a connection, and the bias is the per-neuron adjustable threshold. Calling the whole network 'a neuron' or calling weights 'neurons' is exactly the common error to avoid — precision here is straightforward marks.

questions.q: A team builds a very large neural network that reaches perfect accuracy on its training data but performs poorly in real use. Diagnose the problem and recommend the most appropriate action.
options: The network is underfitting; add many more layers and weights so it can capture the pattern
The forward pass is broken; switch the activation function and retrain on the same training data
The network has overfitted by memorising the training data including its noise; simplify the network and evaluate it on a separate held-out test set with more representative data
The learning rate is too low; train for far fewer epochs so the weights stay closer to their starting values
questions.explanation: Perfect training accuracy combined with poor real-world performance is the signature of overfitting — the network has memorised the training examples and their noise rather than the general pattern, a greater risk for a very large network because its many weights give it the capacity to memorise. The fix is to simplify the network and validate on an unseen test set, the same train-test-split rule carried over from regression. Option A is the 'bigger is better' fallacy the episode explicitly rejects, since more capacity means more ability to overfit.
